manifest.name: chapters
manifest.title: QA Hero study guide
manifest.subtitle: Advanced Level Agile Tester (v2.0) — Chapter-by-Chapter Review
manifest.home-description: Test Strategy, People & Teams, Test Management, Shift Left, Techniques, Automation

chapter.title: Test Strategy and Test Approach Challenges

list.item: Compare test types to be performed during and after an iteration
Explain when end-to-end testing should be performed
Compare the benefits and drawbacks of formal testing and holistic testing
Differentiate among regression test approaches

section.paragraph: Agile testing is not a phase — it is a collaborative, integrated method that embeds quality throughout the development process and highlights the whole team’s responsibility for quality.

list.item: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
Working software over comprehensive documentation
Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
Responding to change over following a plan
Deliver valuable software early and often
Embrace changing requirements, even late in development
Frequent releases with short cycles
Daily collaboration between business and developers
Motivated individuals with proper support and trust
Face-to-face conversation is most efficient
Working software is the primary measure of progress
Sustainable development at a constant pace
Technical excellence and good design enhance agility
Simplicity — maximize work not done
Self-organizing teams produce best work
Regular reflection and adjustment

row.cell: Methodology
Focus
Fixed-length sprints, defined roles, ceremonies, prioritized backlog
TDD, continuous integration, close collaboration
Visual workflow, WIP limits, continuous delivery, flow optimization

key-box.heading: Definition of Agile Testing (Gregory & Crispin)
key-box.paragraph: “Collaborative testing practices that occur from inception to delivery, supporting frequent delivery of quality products that add business value. Testing activities focus on defect prevention rather than defect detection, and work to strengthen whole team responsibility for quality.”

list.item: Testing throughout over testing at the end
Preventing defects over finding defects
Testing understanding over checking functionality
Building the best system over breaking the system
Team responsibility for quality over tester responsibility
Testing is integrated into the workflow, not a final gate
Strengthens collaboration and communication
Testing roles clarify requirements and identify defects early
Quality is a collective team goal — anyone can test at any time
Mindset shift from techniques-only to customer value + adaptability

section.paragraph: Test types are selected based on test objectives, iteration context, user story acceptance criteria, quality risks, and feedback needs. Decisions should be reflected in the Definition of Done and the test approach.

row.cell: Test Type
During the Iteration
After the Iteration
Verifies implemented features meet requirements and acceptance criteria
Expanded integration of features (including cross-team)
Basic performance, usability, or security on new features
Full non-functional testing on integrated / staging environments; broader exploratory and usability testing
Used before/during coding (e.g., TDD); early defect detection
Rarely a separate activity; code coverage may be measured during black-box testing
Verifies new/modified features against user story acceptance criteria (ATDD)
Broader system-level testing, regression, end-to-end
Critical throughout; during iteration it uncovers unknown risks and emergent behavior via structured, time-boxed sessions

key-box.heading: Key Takeaway
key-box.paragraph: All test types can be used at any stage, but how they are used differs during vs. after an iteration. Exploratory testing is especially important to fill gaps between white-box and black-box coverage.

section.paragraph: End-to-end (E2E) testing verifies integration across systems and user workflows. It is valuable but costly to maintain and slow to run, so it should be used with intention.

list.item: Closer to a release or during hardening iterations
When system-wide integration points are newly introduced
When integration points carry high risk of failure
In distributed systems with complex user journeys
When third-party dependencies cannot be reliably tested in isolation
Risk — impact and likelihood of system-level failure
Feedback time — heavy E2E suites slow delivery; favor faster lower-level tests
CI/CD maturity — automation, observability, monitoring, feature toggles, and A/B testing can reduce reliance on extensive E2E

key-box.heading: Test Pyramid Reminder
key-box.paragraph: Broad, fast feedback at the base (component tests) → fewer service-level tests → minimal end-to-end tests at the top. Deviating increases cost and risk.

list.item: Contract testing — validates service interfaces independently; consumer-driven contract testing is effective in Agile environments where services evolve independently
Holistic testing & exploratory testing — uncover unexpected behaviors, usability defects, and edge cases that scripts miss

paragraph.em: Precision and formalization; derives test cases from explicitly defined requirements.

list.item: Traceability between requirements and test cases
Aligned with coverage metrics
Assists verification (built right)
Conformance to standards
Repeatability
Eases test automation translation
Excessive rigidity; restricts exploration
Over-reliance on documented requirements (often incomplete/incorrect)
Misses unexpected and emergent risks
Often executed late → delayed feedback
Low cognitive engagement; mechanical execution

paragraph.em: System-wide view of quality; continuous testing across customer, team, and organizational perspectives.

list.item: Combines process, product, and user viewpoints
First Time Right — quality built in, not tested after
Continuous verification + validation
Collaboration, context awareness, systems thinking, exploration
Lack of focus without disciplined structure
Difficult to measure; context-sensitive
Requires culture change and leadership support
“Whole team” ownership can dilute accountability
May not satisfy regulatory / contractual constraints
Lack of formality endangers test automation

key-box.heading: Complementary Use
key-box.paragraph: Use formal testing for critical components, high-risk areas, and regulatory requirements — automate where possible. Use holistic testing for general quality control, early feedback, and exploring complex scenarios with highest user impact.

section.paragraph: In Agile, regression testing must support continuous delivery and rapid change. Different approaches can be used individually or combined, depending on context, capabilities, risk profile, and automation maturity.

row.cell: Approach
What it is
Automated suite maintained continuously; subset of regression tests triggered per commit/integration based on risk, location, and dependencies. Enables early defect detection.
Frequent risk assessments guide automated and manual regression. Helps decide where automation is unnecessary and human investigation adds more value.

Woven into CI/CD; smoke tests as quality gates, high-priority tests post-deployment in pre-prod or prod. Feature toggles, canary releases, and observability may replace traditional regression in some cases.
Applied continuously or at intervals; supplements automation with tester insights to explore interactions and unexpected defects.
Bug bashes or whole-team walkthroughs of critical flows. Builds awareness of product state and risk areas; useful when automation is immature or as a learning exercise.

key-box.heading: Traceability
key-box.paragraph: Traceability between user stories, requirements, acceptance criteria, and test cases is key. Lightweight traceability (e.g., tagging tests to user stories) helps maintain confidence in frequent releases without slowing delivery.

list.item: Test during and after every iteration — don't defer testing to a hardening sprint. Perform component, integration, and functional tests within the iteration; reserve end-to-end testing for stable, integrated increments.

Combine formal and holistic testing — use formal testing (ATDD, scripted tests) for critical components and regulatory requirements; use holistic testing (exploratory, whole-team) for general quality and complex user-facing scenarios.

Choose regression approaches based on context — incremental regression for continuous delivery, risk-based for high-change areas, DevOps-oriented for CI/CD pipelines, exploratory to supplement automation with human insight.

Automate regression tests incrementally — maintain a continuously evolving automated suite, triggering subsets per commit based on risk, location, and dependencies. This enables early defect detection without full-suite overhead.

Use feature toggles to decouple deployment from release — deploy code to production without exposing it to users. This enables safe integration testing in production environments and supports canary releases.

Run bug bashes for collaborative regression — whole-team walkthroughs of critical flows build awareness of product state and risk areas. Especially useful when automation is immature or as a team learning exercise.
Maintain lightweight traceability — tag tests to user stories rather than building heavy traceability matrices. This supports confidence in frequent releases without slowing delivery velocity.
Reserve end-to-end testing for critical user journeys — E2E tests are expensive to maintain and slow to execute. Focus them on the highest-value cross-system flows, not every feature.

glossary.definition: A collaborative approach where acceptance criteria are defined as tests before implementation, helping ensure delivered functionality meets stakeholder expectations.
A time-boxed, collaborative test activity where the whole team explores the product to find defects and increase awareness of risk areas.
A scenario that occurs at an extreme operating parameter; often uncovered by exploratory or holistic testing rather than predefined scripts.
Testing that verifies integration across the entire application and, if relevant, multiple systems or services, typically focused on critical user flows.
A technique allowing features to be turned on or off in production without redeployment; supports selective exposure and canary releases.
Testing that emphasizes precision and formalization, deriving test cases from explicitly defined requirements; useful for compliance and risk control.
An iteration focused on stabilization and system-wide testing before release. May indicate insufficient attention to quality during regular iterations.
An approach that extends beyond planned test activities to encompass a system-wide view of quality, incorporating continuous testing and multiple perspectives (customer, team, organizational).
A fixed timebox in Agile development during which a usable and potentially releasable product increment is created.
Testing of a previously tested program following modification to ensure that defects have not been introduced or uncovered in unchanged areas.
A development practice where tests are written before code, supporting early defect detection and a stable codebase.
A group of test activities aimed at testing specific characteristics of a component or system (e.g., functional, non-functional, white-box, black-box).
The path a user takes through an application or across systems to achieve a goal; often the focus of end-to-end testing.

list.item: Compare generalization and specialization within a team
Give examples of how to motivate business representatives to perform test activities
Summarize how the whole team approach can assist the development team
Explain how and when to use tissue testers

section.paragraph: In Agile software development projects, people and tools form the foundation for delivering quality at speed. The whole team approach emphasizes that quality is a shared responsibility among developers, testers, and business representatives who collaborate closely. Teams are typically composed of multi-disciplinary or cross-functional members. While specialists bring deep expertise, generalists adapt across roles, enabling greater flexibility and shared workload. Activating and motivating business representatives to contribute to testing through early involvement in defining acceptance criteria, reviewing test results, and participating in exploratory testing enhances product quality and alignment with business goals. This collaboration also benefits developers because sharing testing insights reduces defects and accelerates feedback. Agile teams may also engage tissue testers — external testers who provide a "fresh eyes" perspective to uncover defects early.

The essence of the whole team approach lies in testers, developers, and business representatives working together as an Agile team in every step of the development process. Generalization and specialization of roles directly impact how test activities are distributed, owned, and improved. Agile promotes a whole team approach to quality, where everyone owns quality regardless of title or position.
section.h3: 2.1.1 Generalization and Specialization within a Team
section.paragraph: Agile testers are typically generalizing T-shaped specialists. They maintain deep expertise in testing and broad knowledge across multiple disciplines like coding, business analysis, and DevOps. This supports cross-functional collaboration and enables flexibility in team composition.

list.item: Multiple team members can perform a certain activity
Supports a sustainable pace and reduces bottlenecks
Enhances team resilience
Encourages shared ownership of quality
Essential in continuous delivery environments
Deep expertise in specific test tasks
Required for test techniques, exploratory testing, test automation, usability testing
Testers preserve specialization while developing broader skills
Context-driven balance (complex/regulated domains may need more specialists; lean startups may prefer generalists)

section.paragraph: Agile teams strive to minimize silos by encouraging knowledge sharing and pairing. Mob programming and mob testing blur roles by bringing the whole team (business reps, developers, testers) together to work on a single task simultaneously.

key-box.heading: Testers' Role in Generalization
key-box.paragraph: Testers often take the lead in helping others apply quality-focused practices such as BDD, exploratory testing, and test automation. They learn from developers about implementation details to better assess risks and design tests, preserving their specialization while contributing to generalization.

section.paragraph: In multi-team Agile environments (e.g., SAFe, LeSS), the whole team approach extends beyond individual teams. Cross-team coordination on quality standards, integration testing, and defect ownership at team boundaries becomes essential. Testers often participate in communities of practice to share knowledge across teams.

section.h3: 2.1.2 Motivating Business Representatives to Participate in Testing
section.paragraph: Quality is a shared responsibility across the whole team. Business representatives define product quality and should participate in test activities. Motivating them begins by involving them early in the test process, especially in defining acceptance criteria and formulating business-facing tests.
section.h4: Key Practices to Motivate Business Representatives

list.item: ATDD, BDD, and Specification by Example — anchor the business representative's role in clarifying behavior through concrete, testable examples
Embedding testability into user stories during backlog refinement ensures acceptance criteria are concrete and tied to verifiable test results
Visibility via dashboards — coverage, defect trends, and quality-risk metrics help business representatives understand product quality and where testing adds value
Business metrics — customer satisfaction, support costs, feature adoption link testing to product validation
Shared ownership in daily collaboration — actively shaping "done" during iteration planning and refinement; presence during bug bashes or usability testing
Recognition during retrospectives and demos — reinforces their role in quality and keeps them engaged

key-box.heading: Key Takeaway
key-box.paragraph: With clear expectations, access to relevant information, and continuous opportunities to influence outcomes, business representatives are more likely to see testing as a core part of their responsibilities and a powerful lever for delivering successful products.

section.paragraph: The whole team approach assists developers by embedding quality-focused collaboration throughout the software development lifecycle. Continuous interaction between developers, testers, and business representatives builds a shared understanding of quality goals and potential risks, leading to better technical and business decisions.

list.item: Early test feedback reduces defect costs and supports rapid corrective action (ATDD, BDD)
Clarifying acceptance criteria through examples improves specification by example and alignment between code and business expectations
Coaching developers to write better component and component-integration tests for higher code coverage
Enabling safe refactoring — continuous testing gives developers confidence to evolve the codebase
Automating tests early enables faster detection of integration defects and facilitates CI/CD pipelines
Contributing to code reviews from a quality and risk perspective
Participating in exploratory test sessions together — mutual learning elevates overall competence
Insights into real user behavior through usability sessions and production analytics help build user-aligned software
Early involvement of operations, security, and testers helps design solutions that are easier to deploy, observe, and secure in production

key-box.heading: Result
key-box.paragraph: By embedding these perspectives from the start, developers can make architectural choices that balance functionality with quality characteristics, ultimately speeding up delivery while increasing confidence in the system's readiness for real-world use.

section.paragraph: Tissue testers (also known as fresh-eyes testers) are temporary testers, external to the team, who provide fast, informal feedback on a product or feature in development. They are most effective when used in early phases of design or feature implementation, before teams commit to expensive development or infrastructure decisions. The idea is to briefly expose a new idea, interface, or interaction to someone unfamiliar with it to detect usability defects, confusing behavior, or misleading flows.

list.item: Typically people from outside the team or project (e.g., a colleague from another team)
Short-term involvement — after they have seen the product once, they are no longer unbiased
Their special value as "fresh eyes" diminishes after first exposure
Lightweight, transient nature — like a tissue, use once and discard for that purpose
Early phases of design or feature implementation
Exploratory testing, usability testing, or assumption-checking where quick feedback is valuable but too early for rigorous validation
Before refining an interaction or screen flow to identify potential issues
Do not over-explain — introducing bias destroys their fresh-eyed view
Does not replace structured testing — it should precede or complement it
Primary value is revealing early misalignments between design intent and actual user comprehension
Quick to run and do not require recruiting external users, making them useful in Agile and DevOps contexts

key-box.heading: Key Takeaway
key-box.paragraph: Tissue testers provide a lightweight, transient source of unbiased feedback early in development. Use them once for fresh perspective, then move to structured testing for rigorous validation.

list.item: Build T-shaped skills deliberately — maintain deep testing expertise while developing broad knowledge in coding, business analysis, and DevOps. This enables cross-functional collaboration and reduces team bottlenecks.

Invite business reps into testing early — involve them in defining acceptance criteria, reviewing test results, and participating in exploratory testing. Their engagement links testing directly to product validation and business value.

Use ATDD and BDD to anchor collaboration — concrete, testable examples give business representatives a meaningful role in clarifying behavior and aligning expectations with development before code is written.

Support developers with early test feedback — contribute to code reviews from a quality perspective, coach on component test design, and automate tests early to enable safe refactoring and faster CI/CD pipelines.

Deploy tissue testers for fresh-eyes feedback — use external testers early in design or feature implementation to catch usability defects and confusing flows. Their value is single-use; don't over-explain or reuse them.

Run mob testing sessions for shared learning — bring the whole team together on a single test task simultaneously. This blurs role boundaries, builds shared quality ownership, and surfaces risks that individual testing misses.

Minimize silos through pairing and knowledge sharing — pair testing with developers, rotate roles, and participate in communities of practice. Silos create bottlenecks and concentrate risk in single individuals.

Show quality dashboards to business stakeholders — coverage metrics, defect trends, and quality-risk visualizations help business representatives understand where testing adds value and keep them engaged in the process.

glossary.definition: A collaborative approach where acceptance criteria are defined as tests before implementation, inviting business representatives into meaningful dialogue with the team.
A collaborative approach that uses concrete, testable examples to clarify behavior and align business expectations with development.
A group of people who share a concern or passion for something they do and learn how to do it better as they interact regularly; used in multi-team Agile to share quality knowledge.

A team member with deep expertise in one area (the vertical bar of the T) and broad knowledge across multiple disciplines (the horizontal bar), enabling flexibility and cross-functional collaboration.
An approach where the whole team works together simultaneously on a single task, blurring traditional role boundaries.
A temporary, external tester who provides fast, informal feedback early in development. Their unbiased perspective is valuable only for a single exposure.
An Agile principle where everyone — developers, testers, and business representatives — shares responsibility for quality throughout the development process.

chapter.title: Test Management and Test Process Improvement

list.item: Summarize how to perform test planning in Agile software development
Outline a project test strategy using testing quadrants
Explain how to perform test monitoring and test control in Agile software development
Compare the different types of coverage that can be used for test reporting in Agile software development
Select appropriate test process improvement measures based on metrics in Agile software development (K4)
Explain how to perform test process improvement in Agile software development

section.paragraph: In Agile software development, test activities are integrated into short, iterative test cycles, requiring adaptive test planning that evolves with the product and team priorities. A project's test strategy is supported by the testing quadrants (Gregory & Crispin, 2019), which select appropriate test types, test levels, test approaches, and test techniques to achieve balanced coverage of business-facing and technology-facing needs.

row.cell: Quadrant
Type
Purpose
Examples
Technology-facing, guide development
Verify small pieces of code
Component tests, unit tests, TDD
Business-facing, guide development
Validate product behaves as business expects
Functional tests, ATDD, BDD, prototypes, simulations
Business-facing, critique product
Validate how well the product works for users
Exploratory testing, usability testing, UAT
Technology-facing, critique product
Verify quality characteristics
Performance testing, security testing, compatibility, reliability

section.paragraph: Agile principles further shape the test strategy by promoting adaptive planning, early feedback, and continuous risk-based prioritization to align testing with business value. Test monitoring and test control are ongoing, using lightweight, real-time metrics. Test reporting delivers context-driven coverage metrics. Continuous test process improvement is embedded in Agile, leveraging metrics to identify bottlenecks, refine practices, and enhance efficiency.

section.h3: 3.1.1 Test Planning in Agile Software Development
section.paragraph: In Agile, test planning typically happens at two main levels: iteration planning and release planning. The project test strategy impacts both.

list.item: Team reviews the product backlog and selects user stories for the iteration
Testers contribute by facilitating risk-storming sessions with business representatives and developers to identify risks and prioritize test activities
Define high-level test conditions and refine acceptance criteria
Select appropriate test types, test levels, test approaches, and test techniques (test quadrants can support this task)
Ensure iteration readiness: test environments and test data must be stable and available from the start of the iteration
Include continuous integration and continuous testing practices
Manual testing complements automation for areas requiring human judgment (usability, exploratory)
Agree on or revisit the team-level Definition of Done, detailing exit criteria for stories and backlog items
Clarify how test execution, test results, and coverage information contribute to meeting the Definition of Done
Operates at a broader level than iteration planning
Identify high-level business objectives and evaluate the product backlog for scope and sequencing
Assess quality risks across the release scope and define release-level test objectives
Determine appropriate test levels, test types, test approaches, and test techniques needed
Identify dependencies between features, constraints on test environments and data, and integration points
Help estimate overall test effort using metrics-based and expert-based techniques
Outline a release-level test strategy covering both functional and non-functional testing, how they are incorporated across iterations, and how regression testing is managed
Ensure early planning for experience-based testing and identify where external stakeholders need to be involved
Agreement on Definition of Done at the release level, including acceptance criteria for the integrated product
Define how test results (e.g., coverage) will be reviewed and communicated to support release readiness assessments

section.paragraph: A project test strategy is shaped by several contextual factors, each influencing how test techniques, test levels, test approaches, and test types are assigned to the testing quadrants.

row.cell: Contextual Factor
Influence on Test Strategy
High-risk domains (finance, healthcare, aviation) emphasize Q2 and Q4 — traceable, structured approaches for compliance and coverage evidence

Cross-functional teams with embedded test specialties enable deeper testing across all quadrants. Siloed experts may underutilize exploratory (Q3) and non-functional testing (Q4) unless explicitly planned
Short cycles / continuous delivery demand extensive automation in Q1 and Q4 (component testing, API testing, smoke testing). Longer cycles allow more manual Q3 work (exploratory, usability, UAT)
Low observability → more Q2 simulations and prototypes. High testability → instrumentation and test-only interfaces enable targeted testing across all quadrants
Early-stage / rapid prototyping → Q3 exploratory testing takes precedence. Mature products → greater investment in Q1/Q2 structured testing and regression automation to mitigate change cost

Modern CI/CD enables full Q1/Q2/Q4 automation. Without these, strategy may be limited to manual Q3 testing or isolated environments, requiring compensatory measures (exploratory coverage, feature toggles)

Risk-averse / regulatory → process-heavy Q2 (boundary value analysis, state transition, decision tables). Adaptive / experimental → Q3 exploratory, Q2 simulations. Balance is key: Q1/Q2 ensure repeatability; Q3/Q4 foster innovation and learning
A/B testing, usage telemetry, and failure pattern analysis extend Q3/Q4 into production for short feedback loops

key-box.heading: Key Takeaway
key-box.paragraph: The test strategy should be revisited periodically (e.g., based on retrospective outcomes). As a product and its context evolve, the strategy should adapt by shifting across quadrants, rebalancing scripted vs. unscripted and manual vs. automated testing. The more dynamic the delivery model, the more often this reevaluation is necessary.

section.paragraph: In Agile, test monitoring uses lightweight metrics displayed via burn-down charts, cumulative flow diagrams, automated dashboards, and similar visualizations to continuously track test progress and product quality. These are updated frequently and accessible to all team members for shared situational awareness and immediate action.

list.item: Metrics focus on delivered value and actionable, real-time feedback rather than periodic reporting
Emphasis on trends in defect discovery, test automation coverage, exploratory testing findings, and production monitoring data
Test control is exercised collaboratively during daily stand-ups, iteration reviews, and retrospectives
Data from CI/CD pipelines drives real-time decisions: prioritizing exploratory sessions, refining acceptance criteria, focusing on high-risk areas
Adjustments are made by the whole team, not just testers
Test automation feedback, production monitoring signals, and customer feedback loops are treated as critical triggers for test control decisions
Test monitoring and test control should be integrated into the daily workflow, using cross-functional collaboration and continuous feedback from both pre-production and production environments

section.paragraph: Test reporting in Agile focuses on coverage metrics to reflect test progress, support decision-making, and enhance transparency. Coverage types should align with team goals, support rapid feedback cycles, and be lightweight enough to avoid unnecessary overhead.

row.cell: Coverage Type
Description

Common in ATDD/BDD contexts. Links automated acceptance tests to user stories or examples, demonstrating which functional aspects have been specified, developed, and verified. Acceptance criteria become traceable units of coverage.

Used selectively in highly automated environments. Should not be treated as a proxy for quality — high code coverage alone does not guarantee meaningful testing. Combine with fault seeding or assertion quality reviews.

Reported using session-based test management or test charter completion rates. Particularly valuable for business-facing exploratory testing, usability testing, or risk areas lacking formal specifications. Visual dashboards show what has been explored.

In DevOps/CD contexts, tracks which platforms, configurations, or APIs have been exercised. Monitoring and observability tools enhance operational coverage reporting by revealing usage patterns and anomalies in production.

key-box.heading: Key Takeaway
key-box.paragraph: Coverage metrics should be discussed collaboratively, not consumed as abstract indicators. They must support informed decision-making and adapt to context. Teams should resist the illusion of completeness created by metrics and instead provide tailored information to different stakeholders.

section.paragraph: Metrics-based test process improvement requires careful interpretation of both product and process indicators. Metrics should serve as feedback mechanisms rather than performance targets, to avoid misuse that could distort behavior and lead to local optimization.

row.cell: Situation / Metric
Possible Improvement Actions

Root cause analysis may reveal ineffective exploratory testing or insufficient test automation. Consider refining test chartering techniques, improving session-based test management, or investing in observability tooling.

cell.strong: Repeated test failures from environmental instability

row.cell: Invest in test environment management, service virtualization, or containerized solutions to improve stability and confidence.

cell.strong: High cycle times for resolving defects

row.cell: May indicate weak cross-role collaboration or siloed responsibilities. Adopt whole team approaches to defect triage, involve testers earlier in refinement, embed Agile test leaders.

Strengthen automated coverage at component or component integration testing levels. Rebalance the test pyramid toward more granular, fast-executing tests. Assess whether tests provide meaningful insights or simply inflate metrics.
Redundant or fragile tests (high failure rates, low defect yields)
Refactor or replace using design patterns such as page objects or test data builders.

cell.strong: Declining participation in retrospectives / quality discussions

row.cell: May reflect cultural issues. Coaching for psychological safety and reinforcing testing as shared responsibility. Qualitative metrics (perceived trust, openness) can be as important as numerical indicators.
Accumulating test debt (flaky tests, growing manual regression time)

Schedule maintenance time in each iteration, rotate test ownership, apply test automation refactoring. Measure automation effectiveness by maintenance effort and execution lead times, not just pass/fail rates.

cell.strong: High MTTR or production defect escape rates

row.cell: May indicate missing system alerts, resource constraints, or insufficient exploratory testing of operational behaviors. Consider chaos testing, canary releases, feature toggles, or dark launches.
Low pair testing frequency, few defect learning sessions, or poor cross-functional contribution ratios may necessitate mentoring, role rotation, or community of practice initiatives.

cell.strong: Long lead times from commit to feedback

row.cell: Invest in dashboards, automated root cause analysis, smart test selection, and other tools that enable short feedback loops.

key-box.heading: Important
key-box.paragraph: Any improvement action should be measurable and validated, ideally through retrospectives. Metrics must be traceable to test objectives and risk priorities, not arbitrary benchmarks. Use models like GQM (Goal-Question-Metric) to structure metrics in a goal-oriented manner. Maintain critical thinking and contextual sensitivity to prevent metrics from becoming disconnected from the core test mission.

section.h3: 3.4.2 Test Process Improvement in Agile Software Development
section.paragraph: Teams can adopt structured yet lightweight approaches that emphasize collaboration, context-awareness, and iterative experimentation:

list.item: Conduct focused retrospectives — Use lightweight, collaborative practices such as test-specific retrospectives to identify testing challenges and improvement opportunities. Must be grounded in the team's current context, not imposed from outside.

Establish a baseline assessment — Perform structured Agile test assessments using self-assessments or checklists to identify gaps in the current test approach (collaboration, feedback loops, CI). From there, targeted improvement can follow a gradual, iterative path.
Align via joint risk workshops — Include business, testing, and development representatives to build shared understanding of critical product risks and where test effort is best focused.

Strengthen CI/CD feedback loops — Integrate test-first approaches and pair testing for early, actionable feedback. Use exploratory testing not just to find defects, but to uncover unknown risks and test gaps.
Run small experiments — Use techniques like session-based test management to explore process changes incrementally. Discuss results openly, use retrospectives to adjust strategy.

Leverage visible metrics with care — Monitor automation health, defect trends, and coverage across CI/CD pipelines. Resist benchmarking teams against each other; focus on helping teams understand their own effectiveness.

section.paragraph: Teams can also use practices such as Value Stream Mapping, the PDCA cycle, or causal loop diagrams (see ISTQB-ATLAS). Improvements must account for sociotechnical factors such as organizational culture and communication. Resistance to change should be addressed through inclusive conversations and change facilitation strategies.

key-box.heading: Key Takeaway
key-box.paragraph: Continuous improvement in Agile testing must be rooted in critical thinking, ongoing learning, and whole team collaboration. Each improvement should act as a hypothesis to be tested through action, learning, and adaptation — not a fixed prescription.

list.item: Plan tests at two levels — iteration planning defines test conditions and acceptance criteria for selected stories; release planning assesses quality risks and defines release-level test objectives across the full scope.

Use testing quadrants to balance coverage — Q1/Q2 guide development (unit, functional, ATDD), Q3/Q4 critique the product (exploratory, usability, performance, security). An imbalance signals a gap in your strategy.

Facilitate risk-storming in iteration planning — bring business reps and developers together to identify risks and prioritize test activities before stories are committed. This aligns the whole team on what matters most.
Monitor with lightweight, real-time metrics — use burn-down charts, cumulative flow diagrams, and CI/CD dashboards. Heavy periodic reports are too slow for Agile decision-making.

Treat metrics as feedback, not targets — when metrics become performance targets, teams optimize the metric instead of the product. Use GQM (Goal-Question-Metric) to keep metrics aligned with actual test objectives.

Report coverage that supports decisions — requirements coverage for ATDD/BDD traceability, exploratory coverage via session-based management, infrastructure coverage for DevOps. Avoid the illusion of completeness from code coverage alone.

Run focused test retrospectives — dedicate time to identify testing-specific challenges and improvement opportunities. Each improvement should be a hypothesis to test through action, not a fixed prescription.

Act on metric signals early — declining defect detection, repeated environment failures, high cycle times, and accumulating test debt are all actionable signals. Delayed response compounds the problem.

glossary.definition: A document describing the test approach, objectives, and scope for a project, shaped by contextual factors such as product domain, delivery frequency, observability, and risk appetite.

A model (Gregory & Crispin) organizing tests into four quadrants: Q1 (technology-facing, guide development), Q2 (business-facing, guide development), Q3 (business-facing, critique product), Q4 (technology-facing, critique product).

In Agile, performed at two levels: iteration planning (select stories, define test conditions, ensure iteration readiness) and release planning (assess quality risks, define release-level test objectives and strategy).
A formal description of the state of the increment when it meets the quality measures required for the product; agreed at both iteration and release levels.
The use of lightweight, real-time metrics (burn-down charts, cumulative flow diagrams, dashboards) to continuously track test progress and product quality.
Collaborative decision-making during daily stand-ups, iteration reviews, and retrospectives, using CI/CD pipeline data to adjust priorities and test activities.
Context-driven reporting of coverage metrics (requirements, code, exploratory, infrastructure) to support decision-making and transparency.
A measurement approach that defines goals, derives questions to assess achievement, and identifies metrics to answer the questions in a systematic and traceable manner.
A deployment strategy releasing a new version to a limited subset of users to evaluate before wider rollout.
Deploying new or changed functionality to production without making it visible or accessible to end users, to validate behavior and performance under real conditions before full release.
A group of professionals who share a common interest, meet regularly to share knowledge, learn from experiences, and improve skills in their domain.
A measurement scale and method used for measurement. Should serve as feedback mechanisms rather than performance targets.

list.item: Give examples of how testware can be used as a form of requirements
Explain how storyboarding and testboarding can be used to increase the quality of the test basis
Explain how example mapping can be used to increase the quality of the test basis
Give examples of how biases can negatively affect the product quality
Apply user story slicing to achieve testable user stories (K3)
Explain how requirements engineering supports shift left

section.paragraph: Shift left emphasizes bringing quality activities as early as possible in the software development lifecycle, focusing on improving the test basis from the outset. Testware — such as acceptance criteria, checklists, and automated test scripts — can act as living requirements. Techniques like storyboarding and testboarding help visualize workflows and align perspectives. Example mapping refines acceptance criteria through collaborative discussion. Awareness of cognitive biases safeguards against flawed assumptions. User story slicing ensures stories are small, focused, and testable. Effective requirements engineering supports shift left by combining analysis, documentation, and validation.

Emerging AI and large language model tools can support shift left by analyzing requirements for ambiguities, suggesting test scenarios, or identifying gaps in user story coverage. Testers should evaluate such tools critically and use them to augment rather than replace collaborative human analysis.

section.h2: 4.1 Using Shift Left to Improve Test Basis Quality
section.paragraph: In Agile, testware can be treated as a form of requirements when used collaboratively and reviewed/applied for each iteration. These work products help teams clarify expectations, drive shared understanding, and support ongoing validation. They are often created during conversations with business stakeholders, making them living requirements rather than static documentation.

row.cell: Testware Type
Role as Requirements
Define expected behavior and pass/fail conditions for each story
Translate implicit understanding into shared reference points
Executable specifications derived from team-stakeholder conversations; serve as living requirements and enduring regression tests
Drive development from acceptance criteria; verify stakeholder expectations
Concrete, testable illustrations of business rules that reduce ambiguity
Emerge from high-level product ideas or risk assessments; guide investigation and help identify missing or misunderstood functionality
Define agreed-upon interfaces between service consumers and providers
Living documentation that validates behavior and provides regression safety

cell.strong: Mind maps, flow diagrams, state diagrams

row.cell: Used during Agile planning; translate implicit understanding into shared reference points
In DevOps/CD models, interpreted as evolving requirements when translated into testable hypotheses or decision rules

section.paragraph: Storyboarding visualizes user journeys by presenting expected behavior as a sequence of steps or scenes. This narrative approach reveals assumptions, edge cases, and interactions that might not be obvious in textual requirements. It prompts critical questions about workflow logic, enabling stakeholders to discover inconsistencies or incomplete flows before code is written. Because storyboards focus on user interactions, they help uncover gaps related to usability, flow transitions, and persona-specific needs.

Testboarding is a simple practice that helps visualize what needs to be tested for each feature, complementing storyboarding. In addition to validating acceptance criteria, testboarding shows main and alternative scenarios, leading to early alignment among testers, developers, and business representatives on terminology, system behavior, and test priorities. This shared understanding reduces interpretation errors during test design and execution, increasing test case maintainability.

key-box.heading: Key Takeaway
key-box.paragraph: By combining these techniques, teams create a shared space for exploratory thinking. Testers can pose "what if?" questions and propose negative or alternative scenarios, prompting early improvements in user stories and acceptance criteria. These techniques are most powerful when used iteratively — revisiting storyboards and testboards during backlog refinement and iteration planning exposes changes in scope, risks, or understanding. They serve as living documentation that evolves alongside the product.

section.paragraph: Example mapping (Wynne, 2015) is a collaborative technique that refines user stories through structured conversation. It helps teams identify business rules, associated examples (which can become test cases), and capture questions to deepen shared understanding.

row.cell: Card Color
Represents
The user story under review
Business rules
Examples that illustrate the rules (can become test cases)
Questions (unresolved issues or things the team still needs to learn)

section.paragraph: Rules are grouped under a user story, and examples are grouped under corresponding rules. The team continues until the scope is clear or the timebox expires. When time runs out, it may indicate the user story is too complex and needs further refinement.

list.item: Too many red cards → the team still has a lot to learn about this user story

Many blue cards, or a single rule with many green cards → the user story is large and complex and should be sliced (see Section 4.1.5). Split rules and examples between the original story and new slice; create another yellow card for the sliced user story and put it on the backlog

key-box.heading: Testing Perspective
key-box.paragraph: Example mapping strengthens the test basis by grounding abstract requirements in concrete, agreed examples. These examples reduce ambiguity, uncover hidden assumptions, and reveal conflicting interpretations early. It promotes shift left validation by uncovering gaps in the specification before coding begins. It also supports creation of test charters for exploratory testing and helps identify relevant test conditions and alternative scenarios. The traceability from examples → rules → user needs is preserved throughout development.

section.paragraph: A bias is a systematic deviation from neutrality or accuracy that affects how people think, judge, or act, sometimes leading them away from objectivity or fairness. Biases can significantly compromise product quality by distorting risk perception, limiting diversity of testing, and weakening feedback mechanisms.

row.cell: Bias
Impact on Testing

Leads to designing and executing tests that only verify expected behavior, neglecting failure paths or unusual conditions. Narrows scope of exploration and increases risk of undetected defects, especially in complex systems where implicit assumptions drive development decisions.

Early requirements or stakeholder expectations fixate teams on specific interpretations, overlooking alternative behaviors. E.g., if initial user stories focus exclusively on happy paths, testing may underrepresent edge cases, accessibility, or internationalization.

Diverse opinions are discouraged; testers copy one another's behaviors/beliefs rather than following independent judgment, or defer to dominant voices in cross-functional teams. Undermines the whole team approach, which relies on shared responsibility and critical feedback loops.

key-box.heading: Mitigating Bias

list.item: Promote diversity of thought
Leverage exploratory testing with session-based testing
Foster a culture of psychological safety that encourages dissent and questioning
Use debriefs after exploratory testing sessions
Apply paired testing and tissue testers for fresh perspectives
Conduct root cause analysis to reveal the influence of bias

key-box.paragraph: The risk of bias is increased by the whole team approach. In practices such as Specification by Example and ATDD, failing to challenge assumptions during collaborative specification can lead to ineffective coverage.

section.paragraph: User story slicing is the practice of breaking down large user stories into smaller, more manageable pieces that deliver value, are feasible within an iteration, and are testable. Properly sliced user stories facilitate continuous delivery and enable earlier feedback.

row.cell: Slicing Strategy
Description

Decomposes the user story based on user tasks or steps in a business process. Each slice focuses on a single task or interaction with observable system behavior. Makes it easier to define precise acceptance criteria and expected results.

Begins with a single happy path using the simplest data, then moves alternative data conditions and edge cases to separate stories. Verifies core functionality early and incrementally builds coverage.

Separates backend processing logic from UI functionality when each can be tested independently. Enables earlier test automation and aligns with architectural layering. Build backend stories exposing APIs before UI workflow stories.

Starts with the most common business scenario and builds on that with additional slices for less frequent or more complex cases. Use example mapping or structured conversations to elicit scenarios. Each scenario slice should be verifiable through specific examples.

Each user story includes a complete thin slice of functionality across layers (database to UI). Helps deliver demonstrable value and verify integration. Avoid including too much in one slice — if end-to-end scope becomes unmanageable, revert to scenario or workflow slicing.

Focuses first on general assumptions, then considers conditions from known constraints. E.g., start with a story assuming the user is authenticated, then slice off a separate story for authentication failures. Helps isolate test conditions and improves boundary clarity.

list.item: When slicing, rewrite acceptance criteria per slice — do NOT copy-paste the original criteria into each slice
Each slice should have one narrow user outcome; avoid combining multiple behavioral variations in one slice
The set of acceptance criteria across slices should eventually cover the full original story

Testability as primary criterion: Can the slice be independently validated? Can clear acceptance criteria be derived? Does it support creation of business-facing tests? If not, further refinement is needed.

section.paragraph: Shift left moves quality activities earlier in the software development lifecycle to detect anomalies and defects early, reduce rework, shorten delivery cycles, and improve alignment with business needs. All main requirements engineering activities support shift left:

list.item: Captures stakeholder needs early and continuously
Enables development and testing to start with a clear understanding of expectations
Continuous elicitation through user stories, backlog grooming, and collaborative workshops ensures features are defined, testable, and validated
Reduces defects and rework later in the iteration
Identifies ambiguities, conflicts, risks, and dependencies early
Ensures development and testing start with clear, feasible, and prioritized requirements
Enables appropriate solution design, test planning, and defect prevention before coding begins
Provides clear, precise, and testable documentation of what the system must do
Enables developers and testers to plan and validate work early
Reduces misunderstandings and helps find anomalies before implementation
Agile testers work with free-form text (user stories, epics) and may use semi-formal models (use cases, BPMN, state transition diagrams) when more structure is needed
Glossaries ensure shared understanding of domain-specific terms, especially for acceptance criteria
Ensures requirements are correct, unambiguous, complete, consistent, and testable before development begins
Early validation through reviews, prototyping, and modeling detects anomalies and gaps
Reduces defects, rework, and costly late-phase changes

key-box.heading: Key Takeaway
key-box.paragraph: By shifting left, the team may detect anomalies and defects sooner, reduce rework, and deliver higher-value products. Each requirements engineering activity — elicitation, analysis, specification, and validation — plays a distinct role in preventing defects and aligning development with business needs from the very start.

list.item: Shift left on requirements, not just testing — participate in elicitation, analysis, specification, and validation of requirements before code is written. Defects found here cost 10–100x less to fix than in production.
Use example mapping in refinement sessions — colored cards (stories, rules, examples, questions) make implicit assumptions visible and generate testable acceptance criteria collaboratively.
Storyboard user journeys early — visualize expected behavior as a sequence of scenes before implementation. This reveals edge cases, interactions, and assumptions that textual requirements miss.
Testboard alongside storyboarding — visualize what needs to be tested for each feature. This complements storyboarding by showing main and alternative scenarios for early alignment on priorities.

Slice user stories vertically — break large stories into smaller, testable pieces using workflow, data complexity, interface, or scenario slicing. Each slice should deliver value and be feasible within one iteration.

Watch for cognitive biases in testing — confirmation bias leads you to test only what you expect to pass. Anchoring effect makes you over-rely on first impressions. Actively seek disconfirming evidence.
Treat testware as living requirements — acceptance criteria, BDD scenarios, test charters, and automated scripts are all forms of requirements. Keep them synchronized with the product as it evolves.

Validate requirements before development begins — use reviews, prototyping, and modeling to detect anomalies early. Each requirements engineering activity (elicitation, analysis, specification, validation) prevents a different class of defects.

glossary.definition: The practice of bringing quality activities as early as possible in the software development lifecycle, focusing on improving the test basis and detecting anomalies and defects sooner.
Work products produced during testing that can serve as a form of requirements, including acceptance criteria, checklists, BDD scenarios, test charters, contracts, and automated test scripts.

A practice that visualizes what needs to be tested for each feature, complementing storyboarding by showing main and alternative scenarios for early alignment on terminology, behavior, and priorities.
A technique that visualizes user journeys by presenting expected behavior as a sequence of steps or scenes, revealing assumptions, edge cases, and interactions not obvious in textual requirements.

A collaborative technique using colored cards (yellow for user stories, blue for business rules, green for examples, red for questions) to refine user stories, identify rules and examples, and capture unresolved questions.
A collaborative approach where concrete examples are used to clarify and validate requirements, reducing ambiguity and ensuring shared understanding across the team.

A systematic deviation from objectivity that may affect test objectives, test activities, or test results. Key biases in Agile testing include confirmation bias, anchoring effect, and conformity bias.

The practice of breaking down large user stories into smaller, manageable pieces that deliver value, are feasible within an iteration, and are testable. Strategies include workflow, data complexity, interface, scenario, vertical, and constraint-based slicing.
The path a user takes through an application or across systems to achieve a goal; often the focus of end-to-end testing and storyboarding.
A facilitated discussion technique (such as example mapping) that uses rules, examples, and questions to systematically refine user stories and requirements.
A document recording observations, findings, coverage, and anomalies during an exploratory testing session; provides informal yet valuable insights into evolving requirements.
A collaborative approach where acceptance criteria are defined as tests before implementation, helping ensure delivered functionality meets stakeholder expectations.
A collaborative approach using concrete, testable examples to clarify behavior and align business expectations with development in a format that can be executed as tests.
The degree to which a component or system satisfies stated and implied needs of various stakeholders.

list.item: Explain test heuristics
Give examples of test mnemonics related to testing in Agile software development
Explain test tours
Analyze user stories and epics to create test charters (K4)
Apply exploratory testing to support testing in Agile software development (K3)
Explain mob testing
Explain pair testing
Explain vibe testing
Use test smells to evaluate the quality of test cases (K3)

section.paragraph: Testers must adapt their test strategies and tools to match the fast-paced, iterative nature of Agile software development projects. The techniques and collaboration methods covered in this chapter emphasize flexibility, real-time feedback, and continuous learning — core values of Agile methodologies.

Test heuristics are general guidelines or rules of thumb that help testers make informed decisions, solve problems, and guide their exploration of a system under test. They are not guaranteed to be correct in every context, but serve as practical strategies that often lead to valuable results when:

list.item: Time or resources are limited
The specification is incomplete or unclear
The tester performs experience-based testing
Regression testing is performed without full test automation coverage
The tester wants to think like a user or simulate real-world use cases

row.cell: Type
Description
Examples
Recommended procedures or steps directing testers on specific actions

"Test early and often"; "Testing is about information, not just passed/failed"; "Always consider boundaries"; "Try common workflows and unexpected or invalid behaviors"; "Change one variable at a time"; "Test how features work together in sequence"
Reusable lists of common things to test, used as reminders or prompts
Nielsen's usability heuristics; OWASP Top 10; Beizer's defect taxonomy; IEEE 1044 taxonomy
Unwritten experiential "laws" or commonly accepted truths

"If something looks too simple, it is probably hiding complexity"; "If it broke before, it will probably break again"; "Defects cluster together"; "If it is hard to test, it is probably hard to use"; "What works with one user might fail with many"
Memory aids helping testers recall sets of test conditions
SFDIPOT (see Section 5.1.2)
Compare the system to familiar concepts, then apply reasoning to generate test conditions
Test tours (see Section 5.1.3)

section.paragraph: Mnemonics are memory aids, usually in the form of acronyms, abbreviations, rhymes, or patterns. They do not rigidly prescribe what to test, but help testers recall complex information sets and broaden their perspective during exploratory testing and reviews. They require domain knowledge, contextual knowledge, risk knowledge, critical thinking, and team trust to be effective.

list.item: Guide exploratory testing by providing structure to free-form testing without rigid test cases
Spark creative and critical thinking by prompting testers to think in ways they might not have naturally considered
Achieve broader coverage by ensuring key areas are not forgotten
Serve as mental checklists during test planning or execution, reducing cognitive load

row.cell: Mnemonic
Expansion
Purpose
Structure, Function, Data, Interfaces, Platform, Operations, Time
General analysis of the system under test; useful when exploring a new system or planning test charters
Recent, Core, Risky, Configuration, Repaired, Chronic
Supports regression testing; helps decide coverage in Agile iterations or regression test cycles
Inputs, Store, Location, Interactions/Interruptions, Communication, Ergonomics, Data, Usability, Platform, Function, User scenarios, Network
Supports mobile application testing by generating test conditions
Familiarity, Explainability, World, History, Image, Comparable products, Claims, Users' desires, Product, Purpose, Statutes
Identifies and applies test oracles; detects defects by observing different types of inconsistencies
Tools and Technology, Execution, Requirements and Risks, Maintenance, Security
Helps remember factors that influence the success of test automation

section.paragraph: Test tours use city tour metaphors to guide exploratory testing of a system. The concept helps testers explore software much like tourists explore a new place, using different types of "tours" to uncover various kinds of problems. Each tour focuses on a specific perspective, guiding the tester to examine the application from a particular viewpoint. Tours are informal, lightweight, and creative, making them ideal for exploratory testing, session-based testing, or as test condition generators.

row.cell: Tour
Description
Core features. Bounded by startup and shutdown code; contains the features and functions that users interact with most.
Legacy code and the history of defective functions or features. Legacy code is often poorly understood, and many assumptions are made when it is included, modified, or used.
Features that novice users gravitate toward, while experienced users no longer need them. Mirrors real cities where tourists visit areas that locals avoid.
Supportive features that complement other tours and fill gaps in a comprehensive test plan. Like city entertainment areas, these provide relaxation within the software experience.
Secondary and supporting functions that are often overlooked or underrepresented in a test plan. A place for the tester to step away from primary functionality.
Areas of vulnerability that could be highly problematic if left in the product. Primarily concerned with negative testing, employing techniques such as error guessing or fault attacks.

section.paragraph: Test charters align closely with Agile principles and provide a structured yet flexible approach to exploratory testing. A test charter is a short, focused statement outlining the purpose, scope, and test objectives of an exploratory testing session. It acts as a guiding mission statement or testable hypothesis for the tester during the session. Test charters can be created based on user stories, acceptance criteria, product risks, or iteration goals.
section.h4: Required Elements of a Test Charter

list.item: Mission/test objectives — high-level focus or testable goals for the session (e.g., "Validate user login under load")
Scope — specific areas of interest, test level, test conditions, test techniques, exit criteria, priorities, what to cover, and what will not be covered

section.h4: Optional Elements of a Test Charter

list.item: Actor — intended user of the system
Purpose — an objective the actor wants to achieve
Setup — what needs to be in place to start exploratory testing (e.g., test environment)
Priority — relative importance based on user story priority or risk level
Reference — specifications, risks, or other information sources
Data — whatever data is needed to carry out the test charter
Activities — ideas of what the actor may want to do, what would be interesting to test, and what test techniques could be used
Test oracle notes — how to evaluate the product to determine correct test results
Variations — alternative actions and evaluations to complement the activities
Limitations — what the product must never do
Constraints and risks — regulations, rules, and standards used

section.h4: Deriving Test Charters from User Stories and Epics
section.paragraph: Agile testers analyze user stories and epics to identify test objectives and mission focus. They extract the intent (who the actor is and what their purpose is) and translate it into a mission statement for the test session. Related user stories can be grouped to evaluate integrated behavior.
The 5W1H technique (Kipling method) helps uncover scope, setup, and priority:

list.item: What feature is being delivered?
Who are the users?
When is it used?
Where is it used?
Why is this feature important?
How does it integrate with other features?

key-box.heading: Acceptance Criteria as Test Charter Inputs
key-box.paragraph: Acceptance criteria define what must be true for a user story to be accepted. They can be directly translated into test conditions. While they guide expected behavior, they also highlight areas to explore beyond the happy path. Testers should treat missing, vague, or ambiguous acceptance criteria as opportunities for exploratory testing to uncover unstated variations and edge cases. Acceptance criteria can also serve as lightweight test oracle notes, though they are not exhaustive test oracles.

section.paragraph: In Agile environments, where requirements change frequently and time for analysis is limited, exploratory testing offers the flexibility and responsiveness that scripted approaches often lack. It is particularly important due to the limited details of user stories.

list.item: During iteration execution — to quickly test new features or changes before or alongside scripted testing
In iteration reviews/demos — to explore the product informally while gathering feedback
After major changes — to uncover integration or regression defects that scripted tests might miss
When acceptance criteria are vague or minimal — to clarify expectations and find edge cases

section.paragraph: Exploratory testing is typically based on test charters and performed in time-boxed sessions (usually 60–120 minutes), documented with session sheets, observations, and findings.

row.cell: Phase
Activities
Define the test charter; prepare the test environment; set the session timebox.

Learn, explore, and evaluate the product. Follow the charter intent but adapt as new information is discovered. Use test heuristics and mnemonics to stimulate test conditions. Document coverage, evaluation notes, actual behavior, and anomalies.
Discuss session results with stakeholders, including: charter vs. reality, defects found, questions raised (e.g., unclear requirements), and next steps (new charters or regression tests needed?).

key-box.heading: Test Oracles in Exploratory Testing
key-box.paragraph: Exploratory testers apply test oracles flexibly and contextually, relying on experience, product knowledge, and team collaboration. Common test oracle sources in exploratory testing:

list.item: Consistent with user stories or acceptance criteria
Expressing common sense or user expectations
Consistent with similar features or past versions
Following standards or guidelines (e.g., accessibility or UI standards)
FEW HICCUPPS mnemonic (see Section 5.1.2)

section.paragraph: Mob testing (also known as ensemble testing) is a collaborative test approach in which a small group (typically 5–8 people) tests the same system simultaneously. It builds on the concept of swarming, where multiple team members converge on a given testing problem. This real-time collaboration combines diverse skills, perspectives, and immediate feedback to enhance testing quality, speed, and creativity.

row.cell: Role
Responsibility

Session coordinator; oversees flow, ensures role adherence, manages time, fosters balanced participation. Does not rotate with the mob. Can pause the session or assume any role except driver if needed.
Primary tester; makes final decisions on actions, incorporating insights and guidance from the mob.
Typist implementing the team's collective instructions; does not typically take the lead on strategy, ensuring ideas pass through another person's mind first.
Group of observers who monitor progress and contribute insights as needed.

list.item: Mobbing session — People arranged in a circle with the driver at the keyboard. Roles rotate every ~4 minutes: navigator becomes driver, driver moves to first position in the mob, etc. Session typically lasts ~2 hours.
Retrospective — After mobbing, the moderator silently collects observations from each participant on sticky notes. The team groups similar items to reveal patterns and opportunities for improvement.

section.paragraph: Pair testing is a test approach in which two people work together at a single workstation (or virtual environment) to test the same test object in real time. Typically, one person operates the computer (the driver) and the other observes, reviews, takes notes, and strategizes (the navigator). Pair testing draws inspiration from pair programming (XP).

list.item: Knowledge sharing — encourages learning from each other's domain expertise and test strategies; useful for onboarding new team members
Faster defect finding — collaborative synergy helps find defects faster
Improved coverage — combining diverse perspectives
Real-time feedback — helps refine test conditions, leading to more creative and effective test cases
Better communication — reduces misunderstandings about requirements or functionality
Engagement and completeness — keeps testers engaged and reduces the likelihood of missing test steps
Instant defect fixes — immediate feedback loop can trigger instant fixes (especially in developer-tester pairs), though defects still need to be reported appropriately
Exploratory testing
Testing complex or new features
Cross-functional testing (e.g., UI + API)
Mentoring junior testers or cross-training team members
Time-intensive — two people on the same task can seem less efficient. Mitigation: use strategically for complex or high-risk features only.
Working style differences — different assertiveness levels, thinking styles can cause friction. Mitigation: practice open communication and active listening.
Dominance — one person may always drive or make decisions. Mitigation: clearly define roles (driver and navigator) and switch regularly.
Mental fatigue — constant talking, thinking aloud, and justifying decisions. Mitigation: set clear goals before starting each session.

section.paragraph: Vibe testing is an emerging, informal AI-assisted test approach that has developed alongside the growth of AI-generated code (commonly referred to as "vibe coding"). With vibe coding, developers describe required behavior in natural language as prompts to LLMs, which then generate the code, often reducing the need for detailed human review. This shift demands a corresponding test approach — vibe testing — to verify that the end product aligns with user intent.

list.item: Intent-first validation — focuses on testing what the application should do rather than merely what it currently does
Relies on exploratory testing and AI-generated test cases rather than static test scripts
Accelerates the test process, makes testing more adaptable to rapidly evolving AI-built applications
Reduces reliance on manual test writing
Aligns closely with the intuitive, high-level design style of vibe coding

key-box.heading: Risks of Vibe Testing
key-box.paragraph: Since developers may not fully understand or review the underlying AI-generated code, there is a risk of hidden defects, security vulnerabilities, AI hallucinations, or misunderstood behaviors. Vibe testing mitigates this by acting as a safeguard, ensuring the application delivers the intended functionality rather than just what the AI interpreted from a vague prompt.

section.paragraph: Developer prompt: "Create a login page with email and password fields. Users should be redirected to the dashboard upon successful login."
Vibe testing prompts explore beyond the prompt:

list.item: "Test login with invalid email format and confirm it doesn't proceed to dashboard"
"After logging in, confirm the user sees the correct dashboard based on role (e.g., administrator versus regular user)"

section.paragraph: The original prompt did not specify input validation rules, handling of incorrect inputs, or role-based dashboard logic — vibe testing fills these gaps.

A test smell is a symptom of a poorly designed test. While it does not always indicate a problem, it suggests potential risks and increased maintenance costs. Just as code smells should be addressed to maintain code quality, test smells should be addressed to maintain an effective and trustworthy test strategy. This section covers test smells in manual test cases only (for automated test smells, see Aljedaani et al., 2021).

row.cell: Smell
Description
Solution
One test's success depends on another test executing first or leaving residual data.
Make tests independent, self-contained, and executable in any order.
Test relies on data or configuration changes not specified in preconditions.
Make dependencies explicit and controlled.
Reliance on extensive calling of other test cases or on passing parameters between them.
Avoid nesting calls and chaining test cases; keep tests simple with clear naming and documentation.
Using decision logic in test steps, such as offering alternative actions and multiple expected results.
Make test cases deterministic and branch-free by splitting them, moving decision logic into preconditions, and avoiding "or" in expected results.
Smell
Description
Solution
Test procedures list actions but do not clearly define pass/fail criteria.
Add expected results using a structured format that keeps expectations visible and traceable. Avoid subjective language; establish traceability to requirements or acceptance criteria.
Expected results include additional test steps, often using action verbs such as "check", "verify", and "see".
Keep actions and expected results clearly separated; move verification into its own step; avoid action verbs in expected results.
Smell
Description
Solution
Instructions are vague or open to interpretation.
Use specific, objective language with concrete terms and data. Clearly define inputs; avoid subjective words; reference specific UI elements or labels.
Test steps list every action separately, even when the action has no effect.
Merge steps that have no specific effect.
Many actions are included in a single test step.
Use preconditions when appropriate; follow the "one action = one step" rule with corresponding expected results for each action.
Smell
Description
Solution

cell.strong: Overly Long or Detailed Test Case

row.cell: Test cases with many steps touching multiple features or including the full navigation path before each small action.
Break down into smaller test cases; modularize reusable parts; remove repeated navigation details; use preconditions effectively.
A single test case is used to test multiple alternative scenarios or even the whole system.
Design small, focused, independent test cases organized into test suites.
Each test case explicitly includes all its test data.
Use high-level data descriptions where appropriate; use parameters to separate test data from the test case.
Smell
Description
Solution
Test case assumes specific test environment conditions without documenting them.
Explicitly document environment preconditions and setup requirements.
Test case does not specify how test data is handled on completion.
Define explicit cleanup or teardown procedures.
Smell
Description
Solution
Each different type of invalid input is handled by a separate test case.
Combine handling of invalid inputs into a single test case by using parameters.
Multiple test cases only differ by a few test data values.
Refactor into a single, reusable, parameterized test case while ensuring traceability to requirements for coverage.
Smell
Description
Solution
Test steps are not numbered, with no headings.
Use templates.
Test cases include spelling mistakes and incomplete words or sentences.
Run a spellchecker.
Terminology and phrasing used in test cases are inconsistent.
Use a glossary of standard expressions.

list.item: Use heuristics to structure exploration — when specs are vague or time is short, apply rules of thumb like "if it broke before, it will break again" or "defects cluster together" to focus your testing effort.

Apply mnemonics as mental checklists — use SFDIPOT for general system analysis, RCRCRC for regression coverage decisions, and FEW HICCUPPS when you need to identify test oracles beyond acceptance criteria.
Write test charters before exploratory sessions — define mission, scope, and test objectives in a short statement. A charter keeps sessions focused and makes findings traceable to iteration goals.
Time-box exploratory sessions to 60–120 minutes — longer sessions lose focus. Document findings on session sheets immediately; deferred notes lose context and value.

Use test tours to shift perspective — explore the business district for core features, the historical district for legacy code risks, and the seedy district for negative testing. Each tour reveals different defect types.

Pair test with developers and business reps — pair testing combines diverse skills and provides immediate feedback. The navigator strategizes while the driver operates, catching defects neither would find alone.

Refactor test cases that smell — watch for god test cases, hardcoded data, copy-paste duplication, and overly detailed steps. Apply the same refactoring discipline to tests as you do to production code.

Treat vague acceptance criteria as exploration opportunities — missing or ambiguous criteria signal areas to explore beyond the happy path. Use them to derive test charters that uncover unstated edge cases.

Try vibe testing for intent-first validation — use AI-generated test cases to validate what the application should do, not just what it currently does. Combine with human exploratory testing for best results.

glossary.definition: An experience-based test approach in which testers learn, design, and execute tests simultaneously, using test charters and heuristics to guide investigation within time-boxed sessions.
A general guideline or rule of thumb that helps testers make informed decisions and guide their exploration of a system; not guaranteed correct in every context but often leads to valuable results.
A memory aid, usually an acronym or pattern, that helps testers quickly recall a set of test conditions during exploratory testing and reviews.
A test mnemonic for Structure, Function, Data, Interfaces, Platform, Operations, Time — used for general system analysis when exploring a new system or planning test charters.
A test mnemonic for Recent, Core, Risky, Configuration, Repaired, Chronic — used to support regression testing and decide coverage in iterations.
